coredata.dc:title: Surface chemical composition of chromatographically fractionated graphite nanofiber-derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Vinci, John C.
Colón, Luis A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized from graphite nanofiber starting material by oxidizing in strong acid (i.e., top-down synthesis). The resulting product was heterogeneous with regard to size and surface functionality. After separating the mixture into fractions by means of anion-exchange (AE) high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fractions with unique photoluminescent properties were selected. We examined the surface of these unique C-dot fractions by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and infrared spectroscopy and identified the surface functional groups on the C-dot fractions that exhibited higher photoluminescence. Although some surface functionalities were common to all fractions (e.g., carboxylic groups), others were different (e.g., aliphatic CH bonding). Among the luminescent nanoparticles examined, those with the higher luminescence showed less oxygen content. This strongly suggests that in the mixture of the as-synthesized C-dots, all nanoparticles did not have identical surface functionality, and they contributed differently to the observed photoluminescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface properties
HPLC
Ion-exchange chromatography
Photoluminescent nanoparticles
Carbon nanomaterials
Carbon nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GNFs (A) were refluxed in a nitric/sulfuric acid mixture at 160°C. After dialysis and filtering to obtain a 1–50kDa superfraction, the CNP mix was separated by AE-HPLC using a semi-preparative column to obtain a total of twelve C-dot fractions; (B) TEM image of one fraction is shown as an example.

A) XPS survey scan of the CNP mix and fraction 10 (labeled). High-resolution, fitted C1s XPS spectra of the CNP mix (B) and fractions 5 (C), 7 (D), 8 (E) and 10 (F). The dashed lines represent the measured spectra, while the closely following continuous curve represents the sum of the fitted peaks.

Normalized and offset FTIR spectra of the unseparated CNP mix and fractions 5, 7, 8, and 10 (labeled). Selected wavenumber maxima and ranges are labeled in the figure. Peak assignments are listed in SI Table S-1.

A) Cartoon depicting the CNP mix without any high-resolution fractionation (assumes all functionalities present), and B) representation of higher quantum yield fractions after AE-HPLC fractionation. Colors attempt to represent proximity to light emission under UV excitation and the sizes are relative to each other based on TEM data.
Relative percentage of carbon, bonded as indicated, determined from the high-resolution XPS C1s fitted peaks as per Fig. 2.

head.ce:title: Surface chemical composition of chromatographically fractionated graphite nanofiber-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Natural Sciences Complex, University at Buffalo, The State University of New York, Buffalo, NY 14260, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The State University of New York

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized from graphite nanofiber starting material by oxidizing in strong acid (i.e., top-down synthesis). The resulting product was heterogeneous with regard to size and surface functionality. After separating the mixture into fractions by means of anion-exchange (AE) high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fractions with unique photoluminescent properties were selected. We examined the surface of these unique C-dot fractions by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and infrared spectroscopy and identified the surface functional groups on the C-dot fractions that exhibited higher photoluminescence. Although some surface functionalities were common to all fractions (e.g., carboxylic groups), others were different (e.g., aliphatic CH bonding). Among the luminescent nanoparticles examined, those with the higher luminescence showed less oxygen content. This strongly suggests that in the mixture of the as-synthesized C-dots, all nanoparticles did not have identical surface functionality, and they contributed differently to the observed photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: A variety of emerging carbon-based materials (graphitic and amorphous) having dimensions in the lower-nanometer range, mostly grouped into carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), have continued to gain momentum as nascent nanomaterials for a variety of applications, including chromatographic stationary phases [1], drug delivery vehicles [2] and photothermal therapies [3]. Carbon dots (C-dots) and the closely-related graphene quantum dot [4] are perhaps most promising for their intrinsic luminescent properties for applications such as photocatalysis [5], bioimaging [6], and chemical sensing [7]. However, as the large number of reports on the synthesis of these materials grow, fundamental studies and understanding are lacking, which may hinder future applications and the direction of research in the field. We have demonstrated with various starting materials and synthetic conditions that the products obtained from C-dot syntheses can be very complex [6,8,9] with regard to both size and surface of the nanomaterial.

Although vastly underutilized, the application of high-resolution separation techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) can provide valuable and relatively quick information to allow a better understanding of the complexity of a mixture of C-dots while simultaneously gaining valuable insight such as on-line spectral information of individual C-dot components. This information can rapidly guide a synthetic protocol and allow for the collection of fractions of interest for further study or enhanced application [6,8]. We have found that anion-exchange HPLC (AE-HPLC) allows for the high-resolution separation of many C-dot species with scale-up, fractionation capability and that hidden properties of C-dots can be found by studying the fractionated nanomaterial. While a mixture of C-dots reflects the average of the different properties of the various charge/size species present, many AE-HPLC fractions showed to be mostly single charge/size entities by high-resolution CE analysis. Many simplified fractions displayed unique properties that would have been missed by studying the unseparated mixture alone [6]. Considering the reports indicating that the surface properties of CNPs are directly related to their luminescence properties, both of the as-synthesized material [8] and surface-passivated products [4], it is of high interest to understand and accurately model the surface properties of C-dot fractions of interest. Herein, we report on the surface properties of the previously isolated fractions having relatively high photoluminescence. We used X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to learn about the surface properties of the graphite nanofiber-derived C-dots separated by AE-HPLC. Our findings indicate that the fractionated GNF-derived C-dots with the higher quantum yields were those with less oxygen content.

Nitric acid (65%) along with ammonium carbonate were obtained from Mallinckrodt Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ). Sulfuric acid (96.5%) was purchased from EMD Millipore (Billerica, MA). Graphite nanofibers (GNFs), batch H700, were purchased from Catalytic Materials, LLC (Pittsboro, NC), which were reported to have >98% carbon content and 80nm average diameter. Quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate was purchased from Fluka Analytical/Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri), and rhodamine 6G (laser grade) was acquired through Acros/Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA). A Barnstead EASYPure II water source (deionized water feed) was used throughout to obtain 18.2MΩ-cm deionized and 0.2μm filtered water.

The GNF-derived C-dots were synthesized and treated as previously described [6]. Briefly, GNFs were oxidized by refluxing in a mixture of nitric and sulfuric acids at 160°C, and the mixture was neutralized with ammonium carbonate after synthesis. After centrifugation, dialysis, filtering and sonication, a 1–50kDa superfraction of CNPs was obtained (i.e., the CNP mixture or ‘CNP mix’).

The CNP mix was fractionated by AE-HPLC on a Dionex (Sunnyvale, CA) IonPac AS12A semi-preparative column (9.0mm inner diameter×250mm length) on an Agilent 1100 system from Agilent Technologies (Palo Alto, CA) as previously described [6]. Briefly, detection was achieved on-line with ultraviolet (UV) absorbance at 250nm and with laser-induced photoluminescence (LIP). For LIP, the sample was excited with the 325nm line from an Omnichrome (Chino, CA) series 56 HeCd laser, and the emitted light was filtered with 350nm long-pass filters before reaching a photomultiplier tube. The separation conditions were as follows: flow rate of 5mL/min and gradient elution from 100mM to 300mM ammonium carbonate salt (pH9.0) over 15min, held until 20min, then flushed with 1.00M of the salt. For fraction collection, 11 peaks were identified based on their LIP signal, and a large UV-absorber was also collected for a total of 12 peaks collected.

FTIR spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker Optics (Billerica, MA) Hyperion 3000 IR microscope (4cm−1, 64 scans, 15× objective) in transmission mode on a silicon wafer. Acquired spectra were referenced against an unspotted region of the wafer. Atmospheric water and carbon dioxide, and baseline corrections were applied. XPS measurements were conducted under high vacuum conditions on a Physical Electronics (Chanhassen, MN) PHI 5000 VersaProbe XPS spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6eV) equipped with a hemispherical energy analyzer. Spectra were obtained from samples dried onto a silicon wafer and analyzed with a pass energy of 23.5eV and 0.1eV increment for high-resolution scans and a pass energy of 117.4eV and 1.0eV increment for survey scans. The C1s spectra were baseline corrected prior to peak fitting, which was performed using the PHI Multipak software (version 9.2.0.5). The high-resolution C1s fitted spectra were adjusted to normalize the CC peak to 284.6eV. A Varian (Santa Clara, CA) Cary Eclipse fluorimeter was used for photoluminescence measurements, and UV–visible spectroscopy was carried out on an Agilent 8453 UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer.

TEM measurements of the C-dots were performed on a JEOL (Peabody, MA) JEM 2010 operated at 200kV. Samples were evaporated on an ultrathin carbon film acquired from Ted Pella (Redding, CA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging of the graphite nanofibers was performed on a Hitachi (Tokyo, Japan) S4000 field emission SEM operating at 20kV in the secondary electron mode. The sample was smeared as dry powder on a PELCO tab purchased from Ted Pella.

After modification of a procedure for the synthesis of graphene oxide nanocolloids from graphite nanofibers (GNFs) [10], we synthesized C-dots with a variety of sizes and properties [6]. The GNF starting material was oxidized by refluxing in acid; the resulting solution was then dialyzed and subjected to AE-HPLC separation using LIP detection. A variety of luminescent C-dot fractions were obtained on the basis of their ion-exchange interactions with the chromatographic column. Twelve fractions were collected after separation by AE-HPLC (Supplemental: Figure S-1). Of these, we selected the fractions that exhibited high luminescent intensity on the basis of their LIP signal during the AE-HPLC separation. Fig. 1 shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the starting GNF material and an example of an isolated C-dot fraction. Most non-emissive or low-LIP signal species were not subjected to further study. Our goal was to measure the surface properties of the C-dot fractions from the CNP mixture having high photoluminescence. We note that most studies of C-dots have reported on the properties of the CNP mix and although some attempts have been followed to purify the mixture (e.g., centrifugation, filtering, dialysis), they have not used a high-resolution chromatographic technique for fractionation prior to further analysis. The quantum yield for the CNP mix was typical for a top-down, unpassivated C-dot product (1%). However, after HPLC fractionation, higher quantum yield C-dot fractions were selected out of the CNP mix with up to 7-fold enhancement in comparison to the mix. The fractions having higher quantum yields were fractions 5, 7, 8 and 10, with average size of 10.1±0.8nm, 7.1±0.6nm, 14±1nm, and 9.4±0.5nm, respectively (TEM images can be found in Supplemental: Figure S-2).

Prior to the high-resolution separation of our CNP mix, it was unclear if the surface functionalities measured for mixtures of C-dots were representative for all C-dot particles in the mixture or if distinct fractions of C-dots contained unique functionalities. After we performed the high-resolution AE-HPLC separation, which is based on nanoparticle charge, and no longer found λex.-dependent emission for various fractions [6], we suspected that unique surface functionalities existed on the C-dots, perhaps responsible for the discrete spectroscopic observations for each fraction; therefore, we investigated the selected fractions by XPS and FTIR and compared them with the mix as a whole. Both XPS and FTIR, indeed, revealed unique functionality on the surface of the C-dot fractions. Fig. 2A shows the XPS survey scan for the CNP mix and for fraction 10 (as an example of one of the collected fractions). The survey scans by XPS on the CNP mix and fractions showed that these were composed predominantly of carbon and oxygen, with small quantities of nitrogen and sulfur that can be attributed coming from the acids used during refluxing. The fractionated C-dot samples under study also contained sodium, which was not seen in the CNP mix. As an example, the XPS survey scan of fraction 10 shown in Fig. 2A can be compared to that of the CNP mix. The presence of sodium may be indicative of carboxylic groups at the surface, with sodium acting as counter-ion, or from potential contamination during sample handling. High-resolution XPS analysis for carbon (i.e., C1s scans) was performed on the CNP mix and the fractionated photoluminescent C-dot samples to examine the types of carbon bonding. The spectrum for the CNP mix (see Fig. 2B) displayed extensive signal intensity at the binding energy of carbon corresponding to CC, COH/COC (simply referred to as CO) and CO bonding, with minimal contribution from carboxylic acids or carboxylates. In fact, the C1s spectrum for the CNP mix could be fit well without a carboxylate contribution (see Supplemental Figure S-3).

The high resolution XPS spectra for the luminescent C-dot fractions 5, 7, 8 and 10 (see Fig. 2C–F, respectively) displayed signals corresponding to a relatively high carboxylate contribution, which supports the notion of having some of the sodium in the fractions acting as counter-ions. A comparison of the relative percentages of CC, CO, CO, and OCO− bonding in the CNP mix and fractions shows that the highest quantum yield fractions 7 and 8 displayed higher degrees of CC bonding, accounting for approximately 70% of the carbon bonding in the C-dots compared to about 60% CC bonding with respect to the average of all species in the CNP mix (see Table 1 ). Further, fractions 7 and 8 showed suppressed CO bonding compared to the CNP mix as did all four of the higher luminescence fractions.

The C-dots' composition, as determined by XPS, was also confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. In general, the CNP mix and fractions showed broad OH stretching in the 2800–3700cm−1 region of the spectrum, indicating that functionalities with OH or adsorbed water were present. As an example, a full spectrum of fraction 10 can be found in Supplemental information Figure S-4. Fraction 10 also showed CH2 and CH3 stretching in the 2800–2980cm−1 region, whereas the other photoluminescent fractions studied (i.e., fractions 5, 7, and 8) and the CNP mix did not. Further, the suppressed XPS carbon signal for CO bonding in the fractions studied and their higher carbon signal contribution from carboxylates, which was not found in the CNP mix as a whole, was also clearly shown by FTIR. As the spectra in Fig. 3 show, the most pronounced IR signal in the region of 1680–1780cm−1 of the spectrum corresponding to CO bonding results from the CNP mix. The two fractions that showed the smallest relative CO content by XPS, fractions 7 and 10, also showed the lowest carbonyl signal intensity by FTIR. Fig. 2 highlights the higher carboxylate content by XPS for the fractionated samples than for the CNP mix examined as a whole. Similarly, the C-dot fractions displayed appreciably higher asymmetrical and symmetrical carboxylate stretching than the mix as a whole around 1616cm−1 and in the region of 1310–1400cm−1 for both respective stretching modes; the CNP mix showed minimal carboxylate content by FTIR. All fractions and the CNP mix showed CO/COC stretching in the 1015–1255cm−1 region of the spectrum as well as aliphatic ether stretching around 1130cm−1, particularly fraction 10. Other differences in functionality were seen between the CNP mix and fractions. For example, the FTIR spectrum of the CNP mix displayed bads corresponding to sulfonic acid salt asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching around 1217 and 1047cm−1, respectively; the CNP mix also displayed a band for sulfinic acid salts at around 866cm−1. Such bands were not noticeable in the highest photoluminescent fractions studied. FTIR of the CNP mix and fractions 5 and 7 indicated the presence of epoxides evidenced by a band at small 1255cm−1 that was not seen in fractions 8 and 10. A band at 941cm−1 for fraction 8 indicated that vinyl groups were present on C-dots from that fraction. Lastly, a large infrared absorber around 1440cm−1, which is generally unassigned in literature reports [11], has been assigned to CC bonding [12]. The 1440cm−1 band is also prevalent in the least retained (i.e., least charge) fraction of the chromatogram, indicative of minimum carboxylate present in the sample (see Supplemental: Figure S-5). This provides confidence to the CC bonding assignment, instead of a possible carboxylic acid assignment. A summary of FTIR band assignments with references can be found in Supplemental information: Table S-1.

After luminescence measurements, TEM imaging, XPS analysis and FTIR spectroscopy, it was apparent that the AE-HPLC-derived fractions of C-dots contained unique properties relative to the CNP mix. Clearly, fractionation enriched C-dots species with specific surface functional groups, as evidenced by the XPS data in Table 1. Typically, the surface composition of C-dots has been suggested to be composed mainly of carboxylic groups; this is based on the CNP mix where the properties of the many C-dot fractions are conglomerated together [13]. Based on our experimental observations, one can describe the carbon fiber-derived CNP mix as having a broad emission profile and a range of particle sizes with multiple surface functionalities where carboxylates were at a minimum. Based solely on the observations for the CNP mix, one would be tempted to assign all the different functionalities to all C-dots, as depicted in Fig. 4A. However, after high-resolution AE-HPLC fractionation, our findings show that each fraction had much narrower emission profiles with discrete emission wavelength maxima and unique sizes of C-dots for each fraction. These fractions were composed of a carbogenic core (CC bonding) with substantial CO, CO, and OCO− functionalities at the surface. The percentage of each functionality varied in each C-dot fraction and some fractions contained unique moieties that were not found in others (see Supplemental: Table S1). There are many similarities in surface composition among fractions, but there are also differences. Although oversimplified, each fraction can be depicted separately with their major surface functionalities as shown in Fig. 4B.

The high-resolution fractionation of C-dots, with a separation mechanism mainly based on surface charge, has been absolutely crucial to fully appreciate the many components present in a C-dot mixture prepared by the oxidation of graphite nanofibers. Without such fractionation, reporting properties of C-dots may reflect the average of the many species contained within the mixture. The fractionated C-dot nanomaterials appear to be of discrete sizes, emission profiles, and with defined surface functionalities. The unpassivated photoluminescent C-dots studied were found to contain a high degree of CC bonding and significant amounts of carbon as CO and CO. The C-dot fractions also contained carbon as carboxylate moieties. The fractionated C-dots are negatively charged, as per their anion exchange interactions and CE analysis. The XPS data suggest that carboxylates contribute to the negative charge but the CO content also hints the presence of hydroxyl groups, which can deprotonate to contribute to the charge on the C-dots. The C-dot fractions exhibiting the highest quantum yield (i.e., fractions 7 and 8) also showed to have the least amount of total carbon bonded to oxygen, having the highest CC bond percentage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support for this work by the National Science Foundation, USA [Grants CHE 1058373 and CHE 1048740 (CRIF-MU)]. The authors thank Dr. David Watson for the use of the UV–visible and fluorescence spectrophotometers, Dr. Frank V. Bright for the use of the FTIR spectrometer and Dr. Joseph A. Gardella for discussions of XPS. We also acknowledge Dr. Ivonne M. Ferrer for assistance with the capillary electrophoresis experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2013.08.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanoparticles from corn stalk soot and its novel application as stationary phase of hydrophilic interaction chromatography and per aqueous liquid chromatography
